APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses a core distributional semantics / compositionality problem that is squarely within top NLP/ML conference scope and targets the right audience (compositional vector methods vs. structured APTs). Methodology and evaluation use community-expected ingredients (ukWaC, the Reddy et al. dataset, comparison to embedding baselines, hyperparameter reporting), but the experimental focus is narrow (only the 90-item Reddy set) and reproducibility details/code release are not mentioned, which are important for top-tier venues. With broader evaluation (additional datasets/types of phrases), clearer controls for parser/noise effects, and a public release, the paper would be a very strong fit. | B(4): This paper tackles compositionality detection with a clear focus on distributional and syntactically-aware representations, which is squarely within the scope of top NLP venues concerned with semantics and representation learning. The methods (APT sparse typed representations combined with neural embeddings) and evaluation (correlation with human judgments, comparison to established baselines like Reddy et al.) meet the standards expected, although the reliance on a single small 90-item dataset limits demonstrated generality. Overall it is well positioned for the venue but would be stronger with broader datasets or downstream task validation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a core distributional semantics / compositionality problem that is squarely within top NLP/ML conference scope and targets the right audience (compositional vector methods vs. structured APTs). Methodology and evaluation use community-expected ingredients (ukWaC, the Reddy et al. dataset, comparison to embedding baselines, hyperparameter reporting), but the experimental focus is narrow (only the 90-item Reddy set) and reproducibility details/code release are not mentioned, which are important for top-tier venues. With broader evaluation (additional datasets/types of phrases), clearer controls for parser/noise effects, and a public release, the paper would be a very strong fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles compositionality detection with a clear focus on distributional and syntactically-aware representations, which is squarely within the scope of top NLP venues concerned with semantics and representation learning. The methods (APT sparse typed representations combined with neural embeddings) and evaluation (correlation with human judgments, comparison to established baselines like Reddy et al.) meet the standards expected, although the reliance on a single small 90-item dataset limits demonstrated generality. Overall it is well positioned for the venue but would be stronger with broader datasets or downstream task validation.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper targets an important problem—automatic detection of non-compositional phrases—and demonstrates that combining typed, syntax-aware APT representations with low-dimensional embeddings improves correlation with human judgments over standard additive baselines (reported on the Reddy et al. 90-item dataset). From an experimental-methods perspective the contribution is useful: it highlights the value of preserving syntactic structure in compositionality detection and suggests practical guidance (e.g., when to smooth phrasal vs. constituent representations). However, the impact is limited by evaluation on a single small dataset, unclear code release, and noted corpus/parser-frequency issues, so while this work plausibly nudges the field, it is unlikely to be immediately field-changing without broader validation. | B(3): The paper tackles an important problem—detecting non-compositional phrases—by showing that integrating structured compositional methods (APT) with neural embeddings improves detection performance and that compositionality signals can guide representation choices (e.g., smoothing). These findings are useful to researchers working on phrase representations and composition because they demonstrate concrete benefits from retaining syntactic structure and provide practical insights for when to smooth phrasal versus constituent vectors. However, the impact is constrained by evaluation on a single small (90-item) dataset of compound nouns, limited phrase types, and noted corpus/parsing issues, so while the work opens promising directions it is unlikely to be immediately transformative or broadly adopted without broader validation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper targets an important problem—automatic detection of non-compositional phrases—and demonstrates that combining typed, syntax-aware APT representations with low-dimensional embeddings improves correlation with human judgments over standard additive baselines (reported on the Reddy et al. 90-item dataset). From an experimental-methods perspective the contribution is useful: it highlights the value of preserving syntactic structure in compositionality detection and suggests practical guidance (e.g., when to smooth phrasal vs. constituent representations). However, the impact is limited by evaluation on a single small dataset, unclear code release, and noted corpus/parser-frequency issues, so while this work plausibly nudges the field, it is unlikely to be immediately field-changing without broader validation.

These findings are useful to researchers working on phrase representations and composition because they demonstrate concrete benefits from retaining syntactic structure and provide practical insights for when to smooth phrasal versus constituent vectors. ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper appears to offer a moderate, incremental novelty: it integrates an existing APT-style typed, sparse high-dimensional representation with neural embeddings and formalizes composition operators (⊔INT/⊔UNI) to improve compound noun compositionality detection, which is a sensible extension rather than a radical new paradigm. The problem (compositionality detection) is well-studied and the baselines (Reddy et al. 2011, additive embedding composition) are standard; the authors do clearly differentiate their integrated APT-based approach and provide empirical gains on the Reddy et al. 90-item dataset, but the methodological advance is a non-trivial engineering/representation combination rather than a wholly new theoretical contribution. Given the limited evaluation (single small dataset) and reliance on known components (dependency-typed co-occurrence, word2vec), the contribution is informative to experts but not highly surprising. | B(3): The paper applies a previously developed APT-style typed, sparse, high-dimensional representation and composes aligned APTs (with ⊔INT/⊔UNI) to the task of compositionality detection, and compares this explicitly to neural embedding composition; this is a useful and non-trivial empirical application but not a wholly new conceptual framework. The problem (detecting non-compositional phrases) is well-studied and the use of additive neural embedding baselines and Reddy et al. (2011) techniques are established, so the contribution is best seen as an incremental but informative extension that stresses syntactic structure in contextual features. The paper clearly differentiates its approach from prior ad-hoc embedding composition and demonstrates empirical gains on the Reddy dataset, which would be informative to experts, but it does not introduce a radically new method or problem formulation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears to offer a moderate, incremental novelty: it integrates an existing APT-style typed, sparse high-dimensional representation with neural embeddings and formalizes composition operators (⊔INT/⊔UNI) to improve compound noun compositionality detection, which is a sensible extension rather than a radical new paradigm. The problem (compositionality detection) is well-studied and the baselines (Reddy et al. 2011, additive embedding composition) are standard; the authors do clearly differentiate their integrated APT-based approach and provide empirical gains on the Reddy et al. 90-item dataset, but the methodological advance is a non-trivial engineering/representation combination rather than a wholly new theoretical contribution. Given the limited evaluation (single small dataset) and reliance on known components (dependency-typed co-occurrence, word2vec), the contribution is informative to experts but not highly surprising.

The problem (detecting non-compositional phrases) is well-studied and the use of additive neural embedding baselines and Reddy et al. (2011) techniques are established, so the contribution is best seen as an incremental but informative extension that stresses syntactic structure in contextual features. The paper clearly differentiates its approach from prior ad-hoc embedding composition and demonstrates empirical gains on the Reddy dataset, which would be informative to experts, but it does not introduce a radically new method or problem formulation.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The experiments are adequate to demonstrate the core claims on the Reddy et al. 90-item dataset and compare APT vs. embedding composition, and the paper reports hyperparameters and discusses some negative results (shifting PMI harms low-frequency compounds, parser errors). However the evaluation is narrow (single small dataset, only compound nouns) and there is limited ablation/sensitivity analysis beyond a few composition variants and a single weighting parameter h; code release is not mentioned which hampers reproducibility. Related work appears thorough and limitations are acknowledged, but to fully support broad claims the study needs more datasets, stronger controlled ablations, and released code/data. | B(4): The experiments are solid and include sensible ablations/sensitivity analyses (different APT composition operators, the h weighting, comparison to shifted PMI and neural additive baselines) and hyperparameters are reported, supporting the central claims. Coverage of related work is described as thorough and the paper explicitly discusses failure cases and corpus/parser limitations. The main weakness is empirical scope: evaluation is limited to the 90-item Reddy et al. dataset and ukWaC, and code availability is not mentioned, which constrains reproducibility and generality. Overall the work is methodically evaluated but would benefit from broader datasets and releasing code/data to fully substantiate broad applicability.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are adequate to demonstrate the core claims on the Reddy et al. 90-item dataset and compare APT vs. embedding composition, and the paper reports hyperparameters and discusses some negative results (shifting PMI harms low-frequency compounds, parser errors). However the evaluation is narrow (single small dataset, only compound nouns) and there is limited ablation/sensitivity analysis beyond a few composition variants and a single weighting parameter h; code release is not mentioned which hampers reproducibility. Related work appears thorough and limitations are acknowledged, but to fully support broad claims the study needs more datasets, stronger controlled ablations, and released code/data.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are solid and include sensible ablations/sensitivity analyses (different APT composition operators, the h weighting, comparison to shifted PMI and neural additive baselines) and hyperparameters are reported, supporting the central claims. Coverage of related work is described as thorough and the paper explicitly discusses failure cases and corpus/parser limitations. The main weakness is empirical scope: evaluation is limited to the 90-item Reddy et al. dataset and ukWaC, and code availability is not mentioned, which constrains reproducibility and generality. Overall the work is methodically evaluated but would benefit from broader datasets and releasing code/data to fully substantiate broad applicability.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized and follows a clear intro→method→experiments→conclusion flow, with the APT method and composition operators (⊔INT, ⊔UNI) defined and evaluated against neural baselines on the Reddy et al. dataset. The main contributions are stated early and the writing quality is noted as good; notation appears consistent around APT construction and composition, and experiments report relevant metrics (correlation with human judgments) and hyperparameters. Minor presentation issues remain—some methodological choices (e.g., the effect of shifted PMI, and treatment of parser errors/three-noun compounds) could be explained more clearly and figures/tables could be more directly referenced to aid reproducibility. | B(4): The paper is well organized and the main contributions—the integration of APT-structured compositional methods with neural embeddings and their benefits for compositionality detection—are stated early and carried through the experiments. Notation for composition operators (⊔INT, ⊔UNI) and the APT framework appears consistent and the methodology and datasets (ukWaC, Reddy et al.) are described clearly. Figures/tables and quantitative results (e.g., reported correlations such as 0.74) seem informative, and limitations are candidly discussed (dataset size, parser errors, PMI effects). A few minor presentation issues remain (more explicit figure/table captions or brief reminders of key symbols in the experiments would help), but overall the writing is clear and readable.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized and follows a clear intro→method→experiments→conclusion flow, with the APT method and composition operators (⊔INT, ⊔UNI) defined and evaluated against neural baselines on the Reddy et al. dataset. The main contributions are stated early and the writing quality is noted as good; notation appears consistent around APT construction and composition, and experiments report relevant metrics (correlation with human judgments) and hyperparameters. Minor presentation issues remain—some methodological choices (e.g., the effect of shifted PMI, and treatment of parser errors/three-noun compounds) could be explained more clearly and figures/tables could be more directly referenced to aid reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized and the main contributions—the integration of APT-structured compositional methods with neural embeddings and their benefits for compositionality detection—are stated early and carried through the experiments. A few minor presentation issues remain (more explicit figure/table captions or brief reminders of key symbols in the experiments would help), but overall the writing is clear and readable.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper states clear empirical claims and supports them with experiments comparing APT composition to both the Reddy baseline and neural embedding composition on the Reddy et al. 90-item dataset, and hyperparameters are reported. However, the evaluation is limited to a single small dataset, code availability is not mentioned, and there is no report of statistical significance or broader controls (e.g., additional datasets, ablations isolating the contribution of syntactic alignment versus smoothing). Parser errors and low-frequency compounds are acknowledged but not systematically analyzed, leaving some logical gaps about generality and robustness. Overall the methods and results are promising but additional experiments, significance testing, and reproducibility details are needed to fully support the main claims. | B(4): The paper's main empirical claims are clearly stated and supported by targeted experiments comparing APT composition to neural embedding baselines on the Reddy et al. dataset, with hyperparameters and baselines reported, so the core evidence is sound. There are no complex mathematical derivations to verify; the composition operators and evaluations appear correctly applied. Methodologically, the work uses appropriate baselines (Reddy, additive embeddings) and reports correlations, but relying on a single 90-item dataset and noting parser/low-frequency issues limits generality and raises minor concerns about robustness. Overall the conclusions are justified for the demonstrated setup, though broader claims about applicability would require additional datasets and analysis.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear empirical claims and supports them with experiments comparing APT composition to both the Reddy baseline and neural embedding composition on the Reddy et al. 90-item dataset, and hyperparameters are reported. However, the evaluation is limited to a single small dataset, code availability is not mentioned, and there is no report of statistical significance or broader controls (e.g., additional datasets, ablations isolating the contribution of syntactic alignment versus smoothing). Parser errors and low-frequency compounds are acknowledged but not systematically analyzed, leaving some logical gaps about generality and robustness. Overall the methods and results are promising but additional experiments, significance testing, and reproducibility details are needed to fully support the main claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main empirical claims are clearly stated and supported by targeted experiments comparing APT composition to neural embedding baselines on the Reddy et al. dataset, with hyperparameters and baselines reported, so the core evidence is sound. There are no complex mathematical derivations to verify; the composition operators and evaluations appear correctly applied. Methodologically, the work uses appropriate baselines (Reddy, additive embeddings) and reports correlations, but relying on a single 90-item dataset and noting parser/low-frequency issues limits generality and raises minor concerns about robustness. Overall the conclusions are justified for the demonstrated setup, though broader claims about applicability would require additional datasets and analysis.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares its APT approach against the Reddy et al. (2011) baseline and additive composition on neural embeddings, which are appropriate and directly relevant baselines, and uses the same Reddy dataset and correlation metric for fair comparison. The authors acknowledge limitations (small 90‑item dataset) and discuss when prior methods perform well (e.g., cbow results) and error sources (parser errors, low frequency affecting shifted PMI). However, from an experimental‑methods perspective the evaluation would be stronger with additional recent baselines and other datasets/types of phrases to rule out dataset‑specific effects; these omissions justify a score of 4 rather than 5. | B(4): The paper compares against the canonical Reddy et al. (2011) baseline and additive composition on neural embeddings using the same Reddy dataset and correlation metric, and it discusses when prior choices (e.g., shifting PMI, smoothing) hurt performance. The authors also analyze differences between their APT-structured composition and ad-hoc embedding composition, and acknowledge limitations (small 90-item dataset, parser errors). However, given the topic I would have expected inclusion of a broader range of contemporary neural compositional baselines (beyond simple additive embedding composition), so the empirical comparison is solid but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares its APT approach against the Reddy et al. (2011) baseline and additive composition on neural embeddings, which are appropriate and directly relevant baselines, and uses the same Reddy dataset and correlation metric for fair comparison. The authors acknowledge limitations (small 90‑item dataset) and discuss when prior methods perform well (e.g., cbow results) and error sources (parser errors, low frequency affecting shifted PMI). However, from an experimental‑methods perspective the evaluation would be stronger with additional recent baselines and other datasets/types of phrases to rule out dataset‑specific effects; these omissions justify a score of 4 rather than 5.

The authors also analyze differences between their APT-structured composition and ad-hoc embedding composition, and acknowledge limitations (small 90-item dataset, parser errors). However, given the topic I would have expected inclusion of a broader range of contemporary neural compositional baselines (beyond simple additive embedding composition), so the empirical comparison is solid but not fully comprehensive.

REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper provides most necessary experimental details: hyperparameters are reported, the ukWaC corpus and Reddy et al. (2011) 90-item dataset are identifiable/public, and evaluation is standardly framed as correlation with human judgments. However, important operational details are missing or unclear in the summary (no code release, limited description of the full preprocessing pipeline such as exact parser/version, token-recombination procedure, frequency thresholds, and any corpus subsetting or smoothing heuristics), which would impede exact replication. Given these gaps but otherwise thorough methodological exposition, the work is mostly reproducible but would benefit from released code and precise preprocessing scripts to reach full reproducibility. | B(4): The paper specifies hyperparameters and composition variants (⊔INT/⊔UNI, weighting h) and reports details like use of dependency-parsed ukWaC and the Reddy et al. 90-item dataset, so core model/config choices are documented. The data preprocessing pipeline is described at a high level (building typed/sparse APTs, recombining compounds), and evaluation metric (correlation with human judgments) is clear. However code is not provided and some procedural particulars are under-specified (exact parser/tooling and versions, raw implementation choices for APT construction, and any training recipes for neural embeddings beyond reported hyperparameters), which would hinder bit-for-bit reproduction but still allows faithful reimplementation from the text.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides most necessary experimental details: hyperparameters are reported, the ukWaC corpus and Reddy et al. (2011) 90-item dataset are identifiable/public, and evaluation is standardly framed as correlation with human judgments. However, important operational details are missing or unclear in the summary (no code release, limited description of the full preprocessing pipeline such as exact parser/version, token-recombination procedure, frequency thresholds, and any corpus subsetting or smoothing heuristics), which would impede exact replication. Given these gaps but otherwise thorough methodological exposition, the work is mostly reproducible but would benefit from released code and precise preprocessing scripts to reach full reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper specifies hyperparameters and composition variants (⊔INT/⊔UNI, weighting h) and reports details like use of dependency-parsed ukWaC and the Reddy et al. 90-item dataset, so core model/config choices are documented. The data preprocessing pipeline is described at a high level (building typed/sparse APTs, recombining compounds), and evaluation metric (correlation with human judgments) is clear. However code is not provided and some procedural particulars are under-specified (exact parser/tooling and versions, raw implementation choices for APT construction, and any training recipes for neural embeddings beyond reported hyperparameters), which would hinder bit-for-bit reproduction but still allows faithful reimplementation from the text.

main_claims: Combining traditional compositional methods with low-dimensional neural word representations significantly improves compositionality detection results.
An integrated compositional distributional approach that maintains syntactic structure within contextual features (APT approach) yields further improvements over ad-hoc composition on embeddings.
Compositionality detection is a useful evaluation and can guide improvements to compositional methods (e.g., deciding when to smooth phrasal vs. constituent representations).
_summary.methodology: Build APT (typed, sparse, high-dimensional) elementary representations from a dependency-parsed ukWaC corpus with 90 target compound nouns recombined as single tokens; compose aligned APTs using either ⊔INT (pointwise min) or ⊔UNI (pointwise addition, optionally weighted by parameter h), and compare against composition on neural word embeddings (word2vec cbow/skip-gram).
datasets: Reddy et al. (2011) dataset of 90 compound nouns with human compositionality judgments
baselines: REDDY baseline (Reddy et al., 2011: weighted addition of untyped co-occurrence vectors)
metrics: Correlation with human compositionality judgments (reported; e.g., 0.74 reported for cbow)
limitations: Results reported only on the 90-item Reddy et al. dataset; authors state further work is needed with other datasets and other types of phrase.
Shifting PMI had a strong negative effect attributed to low frequency of many compound nouns in the corpus.
Parser errors and complex three-noun compounds caused incorrect dependency relations for some occurrences.
_summary.topic_area: Distributional semantics / compositionality detection for compound nouns

reviewer_a.name: Experimental Methods Specialist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 10+ years building and evaluating NLP systems, experience publishing reproducible empirical work in ACL/EMNLP venues and maintaining open-source evaluation suites.
traits: insists on clear baselines, controlled ablations, and statistical significance when assessing empirical claims
looks for reproducibility details: dataset splits, preprocessing, hyperparameters, and code release plans
checks evaluation protocols for leakage, calibration of metrics, and whether comparisons are fair (e.g., same data and tuning budget)
values diagnostic analyses that explain when and why methods succeed or fail (error breakdowns, case studies)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on experimental soundness and reproducibility, asking for missing controls and actionable changes to strengthen empirical claims.

reviewer_b.name: Compositional Semantics & Impact Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor in computational linguistics studying lexical semantics and compositionality, with a track record of work on distributional approaches, phrase representations, and evaluation methodologies.
traits: evaluates novelty relative to prior compositionality and distributional semantics literature and whether conceptual contributions advance understanding
values demonstrations of broader applicability and interpretability: how the method informs linguistic phenomena or downstream tasks
notices connections to related lines (e.g., structured sparse representations, dependency-based embeddings, compositional evaluation benchmarks) and expects clear positioning
looks for qualitative analyses that illuminate linguistic behavior (e.g., syntactic effects, idiomaticity distinctions) beyond aggregate metrics
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Assesses significance and fit within the field, emphasizing novelty, theoretical clarity, and the potential to influence future work.
